title: Determinants
summary: How to compute determinants of 2×2 and 3×3 matrices using cofactor expansion, and key properties.
content_html:
Determinant of a 2×2 Matrix

For A = [[a,b],[c,d]]:

det(A) = |A| = ad - bc

Example: A = [[3,2],[1,4]] → det(A) = 3·4 − 2·1 = 12 − 2 = 10

Determinant of a 3×3 Matrix — Cofactor Expansion

Expand along the first row:

|a  b  c|
|d  e  f| = a·|e f| - b·|d f| + c·|d e|
|g  h  i|     |h i|     |g i|     |g h|
           = a(ei-fh) - b(di-fg) + c(dh-eg)

Example (3×3)

A = [1  2  3]
    [4  5  6]
    [7  8  9]

det = 1(5·9-6·8) - 2(4·9-6·7) + 3(4·8-5·7)
    = 1(45-48) - 2(36-42) + 3(32-35)
    = 1(-3) - 2(-6) + 3(-3)
    = -3 + 12 - 9 = 0

Key Properties of Determinants

  • det(I) = 1
  • det(Aᵀ) = det(A)
  • det(AB) = det(A)·det(B)
  • det(kA) = kⁿ·det(A) for n×n matrix
  • If any row/column is all zeros → det = 0
  • Swapping two rows changes sign of determinant
  • If two rows are identical → det = 0
  • det(A⁻¹) = 1/det(A)

Geometric Meaning

For a 2×2 matrix, |det(A)| is the area of the parallelogram formed by the column vectors. For 3×3, it's the volume of the parallelepiped.

Singularity

A matrix A is singular (non-invertible) if and only if det(A) = 0.
